attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll start by reading the architecture notes and current implementation to evaluate refactoring opportunities.

1	# Architecture Notes
2	
3	## After cycle 1
4	- Change: tightened `runScenario` return type from `{ results: unknown[] }` to `{ results: { premium: number }[] }`
5	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — documents the shape tests exercise; `premium: number` field-name contract is now visible at the signature
6	- Naming established: `runScenario(scenario) -> { results: { premium: number }[] }`; field name `premium` confirmed (per example-mapping contract)
7	
8	## After cycle 2
9	- Change: extracted magic numbers in `runScenario` to module-level named constants `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE = 0.1` and `PROCESSING_FEE = 5`
10	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — these are global pricing-rule constants that more upcoming tests will reference; naming them centralises and documents intent at every use site. Rule 2 trumps APP (mass +5).
11	- Naming established: `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE` (10% surcharge on first-insurance, applied to policy base), `PROCESSING_FEE` (flat 5 G added to every quote), `BASE_PREMIUM` (lookup table by item type); per-step local names `policyBase` (sum of item base premiums) and `firstInsurance` (surcharge amount, not boolean flag)
12	
13	## After cycle 3
14	- Change: extracted per-step pricing into a private module-level function `priceQuoteStep(step: QuoteStep) -> { premium: number }`; `runScenario` is now just `scenario.steps.map(priceQuoteStep)`
15	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — names the "price one quote step" concept that was previously anonymous inside `.map(...)`. Upcoming surcharges (curse, high-enchantment, loyalty, follow-up) have a clear home and won't bloat `runScenario`. Mass roughly neutral.
16	- Naming established: `priceQuoteStep(step)` is the canonical per-step pricing helper; future surcharges (curse, high-enchantment, loyalty discount, follow-up discount) should compose inside this function, not in `runScenario`
17

content.text:
All 4 tests pass. Now let me evaluate refactoring opportunities.

**Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)**:

- `priceQuoteStep` — Still accurate; prices one quote step. Keep.
- `policyBase` — Sum of item base premiums. Clear.
- `curseSurcharge` — Clear and specific.
- `firstInsurance` — Established convention. Keep.
- `BASE_PREMIUM` lookup — Clear.

**Current Code Mass Analysis**:

Looking at `priceQuoteStep`:
- 2 separate `.reduce` calls iterating over `step.items` — this is duplication (Rule 3)
- Each reduce calls `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] ?? 0` — duplicated lookup
- Two passes over the same array

**Rule 3 (No Duplication) Opportunity**:
The two `reduce` loops both walk the same `step.items` array and both compute `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] ?? 0`. We can fuse them into a single pass. This also matches the example-mapping mental model better: for each item, compute its base + its surcharges, then accumulate.

This refactor will pay off significantly when we add high-enchantment surcharge (per-item) — having a single iteration accumulating per-item charges is the natural shape.

Let me calculate current mass for `priceQuoteStep`:

Current implementation:
- Constants: `0` (×2 in reduces), `0` (in ??), `0.5`, `0` (in ??) = 5 constants → 5
- Bindings: `step` (param), `sum` (×2), `item` (×2), `policyBase`, `curseSurcharge`, `firstInsurance`, `premium` = 9 bindings → 9
- Invocations: `.reduce` (×2), `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type]` (×2 lookups), `Math.ceil`, `(step.items)` access, plus arithmetic — counting `reduce`, `Math.ceil`, lookups, `?? `, `*`, `+`s: roughly `reduce`(2), `?? `(2), `*`(2), `+`(many ~5), `Math.ceil`(1), `return`(1), lookup access ~ counting major invocations ≈ 14 → 28
- Conditionals: ternary `item.cursed ? ... : 0` = 1 → 4
- Loops: 2 reduces → 10
- Assignments: 0 → 0

Approximate mass: ~56

Let me refactor to a single pass.

content.text:
Tests still pass. Task tracking isn't needed for this single refactor cycle.

**Refactoring Decision**:

I extracted two named helpers — `itemBasePremium(item)` and `itemSurcharges(item)` — and introduced the constant `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.5`. This addresses:

- **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)**: `itemBasePremium` and `itemSurcharges` name two concepts that were previously inlined as arithmetic expressions. The magic number `0.5` now has a name (`CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE`), consistent with `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE`.
- **Rule 3 (No Duplication)**: The lookup `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] ?? 0` was duplicated in both reduces. Both call sites now go through `itemBasePremium`, so when the lookup logic changes (e.g., for rune blocks), it changes in one place.

**Naming evaluation**: `priceQuoteStep` still fits — it prices one quote step. Renamed local `curseSurcharge` to `itemSurchargeTotal` because more per-item surcharges (high-enchantment) are coming and the variable now represents the sum of all per-item surcharges, not just curse.

**Trade-off considered**: I kept the two-reduce structure rather than fusing into one pass. Fusing would lower mass slightly but would require either returning a tuple from the reduce callback or mutating an accumulator — both reduce clarity. With per-item helpers now in place, fusion remains trivial later if needed. **Rule 2 trumps APP.**

**Mass note (approximate)**: Module-level mass rose slightly (added 1 constant + 2 small functions), but `priceQuoteStep` itself stayed roughly neutral while readability and the extensibility surface improved.

Now I'll append to architecture-notes.md.

## After cycle 3
- Change: extracted per-step pricing into a private module-level function `priceQuoteStep(step: QuoteStep) -> { premium: number }`; `runScenario` is now just `scenario.steps.map(priceQuoteStep)`
- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — names the "price one quote step" concept that was previously anonymous inside `.map(...)`. Upcoming surcharges (curse, high-enchantment, loyalty, follow-up) have a clear home and won't bloat `runScenario`. Mass roughly neutral.
- Naming established: `priceQuoteStep(step)` is the canonical per-step pricing helper; future surcharges (curse, high-enchantment, loyalty discount, follow-up discount) should compose inside this function, not in `runScenario`
input.new_string:
## After cycle 3
- Change: extracted per-step pricing into a private module-level function `priceQuoteStep(step: QuoteStep) -> { premium: number }`; `runScenario` is now just `scenario.steps.map(priceQuoteStep)`
- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — names the "price one quote step" concept that was previously anonymous inside `.map(...)`. Upcoming surcharges (curse, high-enchantment, loyalty, follow-up) have a clear home and won't bloat `runScenario`. Mass roughly neutral.
- Naming established: `priceQuoteStep(step)` is the canonical per-step pricing helper; future surcharges (curse, high-enchantment, loyalty discount, follow-up discount) should compose inside this function, not in `runScenario`

## After cycle 4
- Change: extracted `itemBasePremium(item)` and `itemSurcharges(item)` helpers; named the `0.5` magic number as `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE`; renamed local `curseSurcharge` to `itemSurchargeTotal` (now an accumulator for all per-item surcharges, not just curse).
- Rationale: Rule 3 (no duplication) — the `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] ?? 0` lookup was duplicated across two reduces; now centralised in `itemBasePremium`. Rule 2 (reveals intent) — `itemSurcharges(item)` is the natural extension point for upcoming high-enchantment (per-item) surcharge.
- Naming established: `itemBasePremium(item) -> number` (single source of base-premium lookup, will absorb rune-block logic later); `itemSurcharges(item) -> number` (sum of all per-item surcharges — add high-enchantment here next); `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.5`; the local accumulator name `itemSurchargeTotal` (NOT `curseSurcharge`) reflects that it sums all per-item surcharges.

content.text:
Refactor Phase Complete.

**Refactoring**: Extracted `itemBasePremium(item)` and `itemSurcharges(item)` helpers; named magic `0.5` as `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE`; renamed `curseSurcharge` local to `itemSurchargeTotal` (now general per-item surcharge accumulator).
**Simple Design rules applied**: Rule 3 (removed duplicated `BASE_PREMIUM` lookup) and Rule 2 (named the magic number; named the per-item surcharge concept; renamed local to match its broader role).
**Mass change**: Approximately neutral in `priceQuoteStep`; small module-level increase justified by clearer naming and removal of duplication. Rule 2 trumps APP.
**Tests**: 4 passing, 30 todo. All green.
